verses.text: Now the men of Israel had sworn in Mizpeh, saying, There shall not any of us give his daughter unto Benjamin to wife. The people came to the house of God, and abode there till even before God, and lifted up their voices, and wept sore; and said, O LORD God of Israel, why is this come to pass in Israel, that there should be to day one tribe lacking in Israel? It came to pass on the morrow, that the people rose early, and built there an altar, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Guidance: Israel had sworn a rash oath at Mizpeh — no daughters to Benjamin. Now, in the silence after battle, they sit in front of God and weep: 'why is this come to pass? there should be to day one tribe lacking in Israel?' The chapter's question is the right one; the chapter's answer will be appalling. They build an altar, offer sacrifices — proper liturgical motions — and then immediately begin to scheme around an oath their own grief now regrets. The book wants us to feel both their genuine sorrow and their inability to repent properly. Tears flow, but the heart problem remains.

The children of Israel said, Who is there among all the tribes of Israel that came not up with the congregation unto the LORD? For they had made a great oath concerning him that came not up to the LORD to Mizpeh, saying, He shall surely be put to death. The children of Israel repented them for Benjamin their brother, and said, There is one tribe cut off from Israel this day. How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing we have sworn by the LORD that we will not give them of our daughters to wives? Guidance: They remember a second oath: anyone who failed to muster at Mizpeh was to be executed. The two oaths interact — if any tribe was absent, those people are under a death sentence, but if their daughters survived they might be available for Benjamin without breaking the first oath. Israel is reasoning around its own promises like a lawyer. 'Repented' here means 'felt grief' or 'changed their mind toward' — they regret what they wished on Benjamin but cannot ungrant the oath. Vows compound badly when made in passion.

They said, What one is there of the tribes of Israel that came not up to Mizpeh to the LORD? And, behold, there came none to the camp from Jabesh-gilead to the assembly. For the people were numbered, and, behold, there were none of the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead there. The congregation sent thither twelve thousand men of the valiantest, and commanded them, saying, Go and smite the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead with the edge of the sword, with the women and the children. Guidance: Jabesh-gilead had not come. The town's silence is now a death sentence under the Mizpeh oath. Twelve thousand men are sent to wipe out an Israelite town — men, women, children. The cure for one civil war becomes another smaller one. The community that just buried 65,000 of its own commits another mass killing within months. The chapter is documenting the cycle's worst phase: a wound healed by another wound. The reader has to ask: where does this end? The book's answer is the last verse.

This is the thing that ye shall do, Ye shall utterly destroy every male, and every woman that hath lain by man. And they found among the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead four hundred young virgins, that had known no man by lying with any male: and they brought them unto the camp to Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan. Guidance: The criterion is grim: kill all the men, all married women, and all sexually experienced women, sparing only virgin girls because they can be repurposed as wives. Four hundred girls are taken to Shiloh. The narrator does not editorialize; he simply records the act with the same flat horror the previous chapter used. A massacre is committed to find wives. The chapter is not telling us this was right; it is telling us what 'every man did that was right in his own eyes' eventually does to the daughters of Israel.

The whole congregation sent some to speak to the children of Benjamin that were in the rock Rimmon, and to call peaceably unto them. Benjamin came again at that time; and they gave them wives which they had saved alive of the women of Jabesh-gilead: and yet so they sufficed them not. The people repented them for Benjamin, because that the LORD had made a breach in the tribes of Israel. Guidance: Benjamin's six hundred refugees are recalled from the rock Rimmon and offered the four hundred Jabesh-gilead virgins. The math does not work — two hundred Benjamites still have no wives. 'The LORD had made a breach' — the narrator names whose hand was behind the discipline, even as Israel scrambles to repair it. Mercy now races to undo the consequence justice had to inflict. The text neither celebrates nor condemns; it just lets the reader feel the wreckage of every step.

Then the elders of the congregation said, How shall we do for wives for them that remain, seeing the women are destroyed out of Benjamin? They said, There must be an inheritance for them that be escaped of Benjamin, that a tribe be not destroyed out of Israel. Howbeit we may not give them wives of our daughters: for the children of Israel have sworn, saying, Cursed be he that giveth a wife to Benjamin. Guidance: The oath cannot be broken; the tribe cannot be lost. The elders are stuck — and they will not consider seeking the LORD's release from a foolish oath (which the law allowed via priestly mediation, Lev 5:4-6). They prefer a workaround over a confession. The book's pattern: Israel's leaders would rather invent loopholes than admit they swore badly. The next scheme will be worse than the oath.

Then they said, Behold, there is a feast of the LORD in Shiloh yearly in a place which is on the north side of Beth-el, on the east side of the highway that goeth up from Beth-el to Shechem, and on the south of Lebonah. Therefore they commanded the children of Benjamin, saying, Go and lie in wait in the vineyards; and see, and, behold, if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come ye out of the vineyards, and catch you every man his wife of the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin. It shall be, when their fathers or their brethren come unto us to complain, that we will say unto them, Be favourable unto them for our sakes: because we reserved not to each man his wife in the war: for ye did not give unto them at this time, that ye should be guilty. The children of Benjamin did so, and took them wives, according to their number, of them that danced, whom they caught: and they went and returned unto their inheritance, and repaired the cities, and dwelt in them. Guidance: The plan: at the annual harvest feast at Shiloh, the young women dance in the vineyards. Benjamin will hide in the vines and abduct two hundred of them. The fathers can then truthfully say 'we did not give' (the oath language), and Israel will collectively shrug and not punish the kidnap. A whole nation conspires to pretend the daughters were 'taken,' not 'given,' as a legal fiction to dodge a vow. The chapter's verdict is the silence with which it records the kidnappers' success: Benjamin gets its wives, returns to its territory, repairs its cities. Two hundred young women, on the worst day of their lives, become the foundation of the tribe that will one day produce King Saul and the apostle Paul. The text refuses to celebrate. It also refuses to look away. God will work through what He never sanctioned.

The children of Israel departed thence at that time, every man to his tribe and to his family, and they went out from thence every man to his inheritance. Guidance: Everyone goes home. No celebration, no farewell speech, no parting blessing — just the dispersal. The book that opened with 'who shall go up first?' (Judg 1:1) closes with each man slinking back to his own inheritance. The community has buried two civil wars in the space of months. The next sentence is the book's final epitaph.

In those days there was no king in Israel: every man did that which was right in his own eyes. Guidance: The book's last sentence. Said four times now (17:6, 18:1, 19:1, 21:25). This is not a wish for monarchy — Saul will fail, David will sin, Solomon will idolize, the kingdom will split. The 'king' the book is missing is the LORD Himself as functional ruler over the human heart. Without God enthroned in the conscience, every man becomes his own moral lawgiver, and the result is the catalog of decline we have just read. The book ends without a deliverer, without a vow kept, without a marriage that ought to have happened. The reader is meant to close the scroll exhausted and asking the right question: 'who can rescue us from a people who keep doing what is right in their own eyes?' The answer will not come for centuries — and only finally in the One who said 'not my will, but Thine, be done.' The book of Judges ends in the silence where the gospel will eventually begin.
